claimed_contribution: Provides causal evidence that supplier safety training mitigates safety hazards (Study 1) and workplace injuries (Study 2).
Offers convergent evidence across multiple B2B sectors, exogenous shocks (catastrophic accidents and regulation), data sources, and units of analysis.
Shows that suppliers' safety training investment increases B2B procurement selection likelihood, framing safety training as a customer-value lever rather than only compliance or cost control.
context: The paper is set in B2B supplier environments where operational safety is relevant, including a global oil field services company and construction establishments in New York State. It also studies procurement choices for electrical submersible pumping systems in the oil and gas sector.
design_and_data: Study 1 uses proprietary monthly site-level panel data from a global oil field services company in North America (1,921 sites, 87,071 site-months, 2008-2018) and estimates seasonal-differenced IV models using timing and location of catastrophic accidents as exogenous shifts in training. Study 2 uses OSHA establishment-year injury records (2016-2021) for 32,061 New York establishments and applies difference-in-differences around New York City Local Law 196, with additional border-county control analyses. Study 3 is an online stated-choice conjoint with 207 B2B procurement professionals, each evaluating six two-vendor choice sets (2,484 profile observations) on quality, after-sales support, safety training, and price.
main_findings: In Study 1, a 10% increase in monthly safety training hours per capita reduced monthly safety hazards per capita by about 6.45%-9.57%.
In Study 2, Local Law 196 reduced injury rates at treated New York City construction establishments by 0.54-0.68 percentage points (15.56%-18.84%) relative to controls.
In Study 3, higher supplier safety training increased the probability of proposal selection by procurement professionals, with safety carrying about 20% relative importance among tested attributes.
one_line_summary: Across two quasi-experiments and one conjoint study, the paper shows that supplier safety training causally reduces hazards and injuries and increases B2B supplier selection likelihood.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior safety-training research that largely lacked causal evidence on objective outcomes by identifying effects on hazards and injuries with quasi-experimental designs. It also extends B2B supplier-selection work by documenting safety training as a valued attribute alongside quality, after-sales support, and price.
research_gap: The paper states that causal evidence on whether safety training reduces objective safety outcomes (hazards and injuries) is sparse because existing work often relies on cross-sectional associations, self-reports, and simultaneity-prone panel relationships. It also notes limited evidence on whether B2B customers value suppliers' safety training investments in supplier selection.
research_question: Do suppliers' investments in safety training causally mitigate safety hazards and workplace injuries? Do B2B customers reward suppliers that invest more in safety training when making procurement choices?
